question: When initialising the `Camunda8` client, the docs recommend the zero-configuration constructor with values supplied via environment variables (e.g. `ZEEBE_CLIENT_ID`, `ZEEBE_CLIENT_SECRET`, `CAMUNDA_OAUTH_URL`) over passing the same keys to the constructor in code. Why?

options.text: It makes secret rotation, secret management, and environment promotion safer and simpler.
Constructor arguments are deprecated in 8.8 and will be removed in the next minor release.
Environment variables are the only way the SDK can reach Camunda SaaS clusters; constructor args only work Self-Managed.
Passing values to the constructor disables OAuth and forces the client into basic authentication.

a.text: The Configure the connection section says the recommended configuration is the zero-configuration constructor with all values supplied via environment variables, which makes rotation, secret management, and environment promotion safer and simpler.

b.text: The docs do not deprecate the explicit-constructor form; they only flag it as `not recommended`.

c.text: Both Self-Managed and SaaS sections show env-var configuration, but the same keys can be passed in code; the constructor path works for both.

d.text: Authentication method (OAuth vs other) is independent of whether keys are passed via env vars or constructor — the constructor does not silently disable OAuth.

explanation: The TypeScript SDK supports both explicit in-code configuration and a zero-configuration constructor that reads environment variables. The docs recommend the env-var approach because it makes secret rotation, secret management, and environment promotion safer and simpler — common operational concerns that get harder when secrets are hardcoded.
